checks.a_analysis: The HTML output follows the visual organization of the source slide deck. Each slide's content is rendered as a distinct section in the HTML. The layout transitions from a slide-based presentation to a single-column accessible web format, which is an expected transformation. Key visual elements like the North Carolina State Seal on the title page and the 'Evolution of the Mobile Phone' graphic are correctly placed. The 'before and after' comparisons (e.g., Search Results, Notary forms) maintain their side-by-side or sequential relationship as intended in the source.

The source document contains screenshots of tables (e.g., Old Website Search Results on page 16, page 18). In the HTML, these are rendered as data tables with clear borders, distinct headers, and aligned columns. For example, the 'Search Results' table from page 16/18 is accurately reconstructed as an HTML table that a sighted user can easily read and understand. The 'Contact Lobbying' table on page 26 is also correctly rendered.

Meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML output. This includes the State Seal on the title slide, the abstract digital background on page 4, the accessibility icons on page 5, the 'Evolution of the Mobile Phone' chart on page 8, and the various screenshots of the website redesign. Decorative elements like the orange line under headings are omitted for accessibility, which is correct behavior.

The HTML output does not contain duplicated body content. While titles and headings appear in the Table of Contents and again in the main body, this is expected accessible design. The actual slide content (text, tables, images) is rendered once per section, corresponding to the pages of the source PDF.
